rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is established by the parent statement: measures are presented "to achieve the climate goals by 2030." The policy statement itself is an enabling finance mechanism: "Develop a City-wide forum to coordinate applications for external financial support (2024–2030)." Although no direct mitigation outcome token appears (hence the gate failure), the action mobilizes/coordinates external financing that supports implementation of climate measures under the parent climate-goals program. Keeping as Mitigation is acceptable as an enabling measure for mitigation delivery.
policy_statement: Develop a City-wide forum to coordinate applications for external financial support (2024–2030).
mechanism_description: Developing a city-wide forum coordinates applications for external financial support, helping mobilize funding to implement climate actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Finance coordination/governance → ↑ access to external capital → implementation of emissions-reducing measures → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human/system activity: The city creates a coordinating forum/process to identify, package, and submit applications for grants/loans and align partners around external funding opportunities. (2) Mediating process: Reduced transaction costs and improved project pipeline/bankability increases success in securing external finance and accelerates deployment of funded interventions. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Earlier and larger-scale deployment of climate measures leads to net reductions in citywide GHG emissions (with possible co-benefits for resilience depending on what is funded).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is mobilizing external finance that enables implementation of climate projects that (most often) reduce GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/coordination increases access to external finance, which enables implementation of climate projects; the most common climate-dominant outcome of such enabled projects is emissions reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism is not itself an adaptation measure or a resource-efficiency intervention; it is an enabling lever whose typical primary climate payoff is mitigation via financed decarbonization investments.  
3) Edge cases applied: if the forum is explicitly limited to resilience funding (e.g., flood defenses) the dominant pathway would be adaptation; if limited to ecosystem restoration finance, it could be nature-based solutions. Those constraints are not stated here.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: all “external finance coordination forum → climate investment mobilization” policies operate by mobilizing funds for climate action implementation; absent an explicit restriction, mitigation is the most defensible default dominant pathway for the mechanism class.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether specific funded actions are mitigation vs adaptation/resource efficiency/NBS must be assigned at the policy/project level based on evidence of what investments are financed.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Enabling-finance mechanisms are cross-cutting: the correct primary category can flip to Adaptation or Nature-Based Solutions if the coordination forum is explicitly tied to resilience-only or ecosystem-only funding; classification should be revisited if the policy text specifies the funded project types.
key_indicators: "Develop a City-wide forum"; "coordination of applications"; "external financial support"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an incentive-program if it included a defined financing facility or co-financing commitments; however, the text only specifies coordination/fora, so it fits best as a planning/strategy governance instrument.
